author: @amazac2078
text: hiya Rob,thanks for sharing that was awesome! i think inkjet printers cannot handle your ink due to its contents and viscosity but for sure a 3D resin printer will do a great job there are many cheap ones available in the market ,fantastic idea !for future tons of applications,absolutely future will be so bright for such an ink.well done ! cheers mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text: Either an ink-jet or bubble-jet printer would seem a more efficient way of printing... ~( 'w')/

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: won't work with the ink - see my comment to Ama Zac

replies.author: @DeliciousDeBlair
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Oh... too bad.

Well, maybe if one printed a mask [or nonstick film] and then used a litho type roller process.

Would certainly speed up normal battery/capacitor manufacture to use an offset ink press method.

In fact, if you need it in a pattern, just make a template, roll the ink on to your current collector, then run that right through a laser cutter.

If you use continuous feed, you can greatly reduce material costs by using nesting to maximize material efficiency.

One can pretty much work it just like a printing press. I have worked them and also other chain process machinery, could build one cheaply even. [assuming one could not be gotten cheaply enough used at an auction]

author: @akvalohs
text:
What is the viscosity of the conductive ink? I'm thinking if by printing via inkjet would be better, since that way it would minimize waste and 'skip' the peeling-off step. 
Although, in hindsight, that looks like an ink that would easily ruin some printheads

author: @olivierroy1301
text: How do I pass from the ink to a weldable tab?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the components are held on by the ink - it acts like a glue - but if you wanted to attach a tab you would glue the metal onto the ink then overpaint the section that laying on the ink and hey presto - maybe I'll do demo - sometimes so much easier than words lol

replies.author: @zylascope
replies.text: You could screw the metal tab onto the board, using a nut and bolt, and paint the ink over it. And also have the ink go under the metal tab.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @zylascope nice idea mate - cheers
